news_summary: Recent breakthroughs reveal natural and innovative solutions to environmental challenges, including volcanic methane reduction, safe termite control, and strategies to protect ocean currents, while celebrating conservation efforts inspired by David Attenborough.
news_summary_fr: Des avancées récentes mettent en lumière des solutions naturelles et innovantes aux défis environnementaux, notamment la réduction des émissions de méthane d'origine volcanique, la lutte sans danger contre les termites et les stratégies visant à protéger les courants océaniques, tout en rendant hommage aux initiatives de préservation inspirées par David Attenborough.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances revelan soluciones naturales e innovadoras a los retos medioambientales, como la reducción del metano volcánico, el control seguro de las termitas y las estrategias para proteger las corrientes oceánicas, al tiempo que se rinden homenaje a los esfuerzos de conservación inspirados por David Attenborough.

articles.title: Scientists stunned as volcano cloud destroys methane in the atmosphere
articles.summary: A colossal underwater volcano in the South Pacific may have revealed a surprising new weapon against climate change. After the 2022 eruption of Hunga Tonga–Hunga Ha’apai, scientists detected enormous amounts of formaldehyde in the atmosphere — a telltale sign that methane, one of the planet’s most powerful greenhouse gases, was being destroyed. Researchers now believe volcanic ash mixed with salty seawater and sunlight created reactive chlorine particles that effectively “cleaned up” some of the methane released by the eruption itself.
articles.content:
In January 2022, the underwater volcano Hunga Tonga-Hunga Ha'apai in the South Pacific produced one of the most powerful eruptions in modern history. But scientists have now discovered that the eruption also triggered an unexpected atmospheric reaction that partially removed methane, a potent greenhouse gas, from the air. Researchers say the finding could eventually help scientists develop new strategies to slow global warming.

Using satellite observations, scientists detected unusually high levels of formaldehyde inside the enormous volcanic plume created by the eruption. That discovery immediately caught their attention because formaldehyde is produced when methane breaks down in the atmosphere.

"When we analyzed the satellite images, we were surprised to see a cloud with a record-high concentration of formaldehyde. We were able to track the cloud for 10 days, all the way to South America. Because formaldehyde only exists for a few hours, this showed that the cloud must have been destroying methane continuously for more than a week," explains Dr. Maarten van Herpen from Acacia Impact Innovation BV, first author of the study, which has just been published in Nature Communications.

"It is known that volcanoes emit methane during eruptions, but until now it was not known that volcanic ash is also capable of partially cleaning up this pollution," he adds.

Volcano Ash, Sea Salt, and Sunlight Triggered Chemical Reaction

The researchers believe the eruption activated a rare chemical process that they had previously identified in an entirely different environment.

In earlier research published in 2023, scientists discovered that dust blowing from the Sahara Desert across the Atlantic Ocean can combine with salt from sea spray to create tiny particles called iron salt aerosols. When sunlight strikes these particles, chlorine atoms are released. Those chlorine atoms react with methane and help break it apart in the atmosphere. The discovery significantly changed scientists' understanding of atmospheric chemistry in the troposphere.

"What is new -- and completely surprising -- is that the same mechanism appears to occur in a volcanic plume high up in the stratosphere, where the physical conditions are entirely different," says Professor Matthew Johnson from the Department of Chemistry at the University of Copenhagen, one of the researchers behind both discoveries.

During the Tonga eruption, massive amounts of salty seawater were blasted into the stratosphere together with volcanic ash. Researchers think sunlight interacting with this mixture created highly reactive chlorine that then helped destroy methane released during the eruption. The unusually high formaldehyde levels detected by satellites served as evidence that methane breakdown was taking place.

Scientists Say Global Methane Estimates May Need Revision The discovery also suggests that scientists may need to rethink the global methane budget, which estimates how much methane enters and leaves Earth's atmosphere. "We now know that atmospheric dust -- for example from a volcanic eruption -- impacts the methane budget, meaning the budget of how much methane is added to the atmosphere and how much is removed. Because dust has not previously been taken into account, it is important that we correct the data on which these estimates are based," says Matthew Johnson.

Why Methane Matters for Climate Change

Methane is responsible for about one third of current global warming. Over a 20-year period, methane traps roughly 80 times more heat than CO 2 . Unlike carbon dioxide, however, methane does not remain in the atmosphere for centuries. It typically breaks down within about 10 years.

Because methane has a shorter atmospheric lifetime, reducing methane pollution could produce climate benefits relatively quickly. Scientists sometimes describe methane reduction as an "emergency brake" for climate change because lowering methane levels could help slow warming within the next decade and potentially reduce the risk of climate tipping points. Researchers stress, however, that cutting CO 2 emissions remains critical for long-term climate stability.

Discovery Could Inspire Future Climate Technologies

The team says the findings may help advance efforts to artificially accelerate methane removal from the atmosphere. Scientists around the world are currently exploring several possible approaches, but accurately measuring methane removal has been a major challenge.

"How do you prove that methane has been removed from the atmosphere? How do you know your method works? It's very difficult. But here we address that problem by showing that methane breakdown can in fact be observed using satellites," says Dr. Jos de Laat from the Royal Netherlands Meteorological Institute, senior author of the study.

The research relied on the TROPOMI instrument aboard the European Space Agency's Sentinel-5P satellite, which tracks greenhouse gases and air pollution around the globe every day.

"Retrieving formaldehyde from TROPOMI in a stratospheric volcanic plume is far outside the instrument's standard operating conditions -- we had to carefully correct the satellite's sensitivity for the unusual altitude of the signal and account for interference from the high sulfur dioxide concentrations. Getting these corrections right was essential to confirm that what we were seeing was real," said Dr. Isabelle De Smedt, Royal Belgian Institute for Space Aeronomy.

Researchers believe the discovery could eventually inspire practical engineering solutions aimed at reducing methane pollution.

"It's an obvious idea for industry to try to replicate this natural phenomenon ­ -- but only if it can be proven to be safe and effective. Our satellite method could offer a way to help figure out how humans might slow global warming," concludes Matthew Johnson.

About the Study
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery showing that volcanic eruptions can naturally destroy methane, a potent greenhouse gas, through a chemical reaction involving volcanic ash, seawater, and sunlight. This finding has broad implications for understanding and potentially mitigating climate change by improving methane budget estimates and inspiring new methane removal technologies. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a breakthrough with meaningful environmental impact.
articles.personality_title: Underwater volcano eruption helps break down methane in the atmosphere
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In January 2022, the underwater volcano Hunga Tonga–Hunga Ha’apai in the South Pacific erupted powerfully. Methane is a greenhouse gas that traps heat in the atmosphere and contributes to global warming.

**What happened** – After the eruption, scientists used satellites to detect very high levels of formaldehyde in the volcanic plume. Formaldehyde forms when methane breaks down, showing that methane was being destroyed. They found that volcanic ash mixed with salty seawater and sunlight created chlorine particles. These particles helped break down methane released by the volcano.

**Impact** – This is the first time scientists have seen volcanic ash help reduce methane in the atmosphere. It changes how we understand how methane moves and breaks down on Earth. Since methane causes a lot of warming over a short time, this natural “cleanup” could be important for climate research and action.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to study this reaction more and see if it can be safely used to reduce methane pollution on purpose. They also plan to update global methane estimates to include this process, which could improve climate models and strategies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A volcanic eruption naturally created chlorine particles that helped destroy methane in the atmosphere, revealing a new way methane can be reduced and tracked from space.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’éruption d’un volcan sous-marin aide à détruire le méthane dans l’atmosphère
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En janvier 2022, le volcan sous-marin Hunga Tonga–Hunga Ha’apai dans le Pacifique Sud a connu une puissante éruption. Le méthane est un gaz à effet de serre qui retient la chaleur dans l’atmosphère et participe au réchauffement climatique.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Après l’éruption, des scientifiques ont détecté par satellite des niveaux très élevés de formaldéhyde dans le panache volcanique. Le formaldéhyde se forme lorsque le méthane se décompose, ce qui montre que le méthane était détruit. Ils ont découvert que les cendres volcaniques mélangées à l’eau de mer salée et à la lumière du soleil créaient des particules de chlore. Ces particules ont aidé à décomposer le méthane libéré par le volcan.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que les scientifiques observent que les cendres volcaniques contribuent à réduire le méthane dans l’atmosphère. Cela modifie notre compréhension du cycle du méthane sur Terre. Comme le méthane cause beaucoup de réchauffement sur une courte période, ce « nettoyage » naturel pourrait avoir un rôle important pour la recherche et les actions climatiques.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent étudier davantage cette réaction et voir si elle peut être utilisée en toute sécurité pour réduire la pollution au méthane. Ils prévoient aussi de mettre à jour les estimations mondiales du méthane pour inclure ce processus, ce qui pourrait améliorer les modèles climatiques et les stratégies.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une éruption volcanique a naturellement créé des particules de chlore qui ont aidé à détruire le méthane dans l’atmosphère, révélant une nouvelle façon de réduire et de suivre le méthane depuis l’espace.
articles.personality_title_es: La erupción de un volcán submarino ayuda a descomponer el metano en la atmósfera
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En enero de 2022, el volcán submarino Hunga Tonga–Hunga Ha’apai en el Pacífico Sur tuvo una erupción muy fuerte. El metano es un gas de efecto invernadero que atrapa el calor en la atmósfera y contribuye al calentamiento global.

**Qué pasó** – Después de la erupción, los científicos detectaron con satélites niveles muy altos de formaldehído en la nube volcánica. El formaldehído se forma cuando el metano se descompone, lo que mostró que el metano estaba siendo destruido. Descubrieron que las cenizas volcánicas mezcladas con agua salada del mar y la luz del sol crearon partículas de cloro. Estas partículas ayudaron a romper el metano liberado por el volcán.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que se observa que las cenizas volcánicas ayudan a reducir el metano en la atmósfera. Esto cambia cómo entendemos el ciclo del metano en la Tierra. Como el metano causa mucho calentamiento en poco tiempo, esta “limpieza” natural podría ser importante para la investigación y acción climática.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren estudiar más esta reacción y ver si puede usarse de manera segura para reducir la contaminación por metano. También planean actualizar las estimaciones globales de metano para incluir este proceso, mejorando los modelos y estrategias climáticas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una erupción volcánica creó partículas de cloro que ayudaron a destruir el metano en la atmósfera, mostrando una nueva forma de reducir y monitorear el metano desde el espacio.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, detailed painting of a towering underwater volcano erupting beneath a calm South Pacific ocean, sending a luminous plume of ash and mist high into the sky, where swirling clouds infused with glowing particles symbolizing reactive chlorine gently dissolve stylized methane molecules represented as soft, translucent bubbles, all bathed in warm, natural hues of blues, oranges, and soft whites to evoke hope and atmospheric transformation.

articles.title: New chemical kills 95% of termites without harming humans
articles.summary: Scientists may have found a smarter, safer way to wipe out termites hiding inside homes. A chemical called bistrifluron prevents drywood termites from forming new exoskeletons during molting, killing entire colonies from within. In tests, it eliminated about 95% of termites while avoiding the toxic side effects of traditional fumigation. Researchers say the method could provide longer-lasting protection as termites spread into new areas.
articles.content:
Drywood termites are experts at staying out of sight. They live inside wooden structures, quietly feeding and expanding their colonies where homeowners may not notice them until damage is already underway. But their hidden lifestyle also depends on a vulnerable biological process: molting.

Researchers at the University of California, Riverside have shown that bistrifluron, a chemical that blocks the formation of new termite exoskeletons, can destroy drywood termite colonies by interfering with the insects' ability to grow. The findings were published in the Journal of Economic Entomology. In laboratory testing, the treatment killed about 95 percent of a colony without the mammal toxicity concerns linked to many traditional termite control methods.

A Safer Way To Target Termites

"This chemical is more environmentally friendly than ones traditionally used for drywood termite infestations," said Nicholas Poulos, corresponding author of the paper and a doctoral student in UCR's Department of Entomology. "It's specific to insects and can't harm humans."

The reason the chemical is so targeted comes down to the termite body plan. Humans have bones inside their bodies. Termites wear their support system on the outside in the form of an exoskeleton. That outer shell is built largely from chitin, a tough natural material also found in fungal cell walls, fish scales, and the beaks of squids and octopi.

For insects, chitin is essential. It gives the exoskeleton strength, helps shield the body, and provides anchoring points for muscles. When termites grow, they must shed their old exoskeleton and build a new one. Drywood termites go through this process about seven times during their lives.

Bistrifluron interrupts that step. Instead of poisoning termites in a broad, fast acting way, it prevents them from making the chitin they need for their next protective shell.

"Once the termites reach a certain stage, they have to molt. They cannot avoid that," said Dong-Hwan Choe, UCR entomology professor and senior paper author. "With a lethal dose of this chemical, they'll try to shed their old exoskeleton but won't have a new one ready to protect them."

Termites Spread the Treatment Themselves

The effect is not instant. The researchers saw that bistrifluron first made the termites less active and reduced their feeding. Over time, the chemical blocked successful molting, and the insects died.

The 2025 study tested three chitin synthesis inhibitors against the western drywood termite, Incisitermes minor. Bistrifluron worked faster than chlorfluazuron and noviflumuron at the tested rates. In one no choice test, bistrifluron produced 99 percent mortality over 60 days. In a choice test using a 0.1 percent rate, it produced 96 percent mortality over the same period.

The most important part may be how the chemical travels. After termites fed on treated wood, they passed material to other members of the colony. In transfer tests, even when only 5 percent of termites had been exposed, the groups reached 100 percent mortality by day 90. The study reported that food material moved from exposed donor termites to unexposed recipients within 24 to 48 hours.

That finding fits with newer UC Riverside work highlighting how western drywood termites share food and essential gut microbes through proctodeal trophallaxis, a mouth to anus feeding behavior that is difficult to observe because the insects live almost entirely inside wood. Those hidden social behaviors can make infestations hard to detect, but they may also help treatments spread once termites contact treated material.

"It's been successfully used on subterranean termites, which are also important structural pests," Choe said. "But native western drywood termites are also important, especially in California."

A Slower Collapse With Big Advantages

Once drywood termites consume the treated wood, the compound can move through the colony as termites interact. Full colony collapse takes roughly two months, making it slower than some conventional methods. But the tradeoff could be worth it: lower toxicity, more targeted action, and the potential for a localized treatment that does not require tenting an entire home.

"We believe this method of spot treatment can kill a larger colony and spread more easily than current termite control methods," Choe said. "You don't have to apply too much to get a very good result. The chitin synthesis inhibitors show promise as localized treatment for drywood termites."

Traditional fumigation remains a major burden for homeowners. It can be toxic, disruptive, and stressful. People often have to bag food, leave the house, and wait before returning. It also does not prevent termites from coming back later.

"Low-impact strategies like this one will become an attractive option in many cases. Furthermore, the chemical may stay active in the wood for some time, potentially providing protection from future infestations," Choe said.

A Chemical Lure Could Make Treatment Stronger

The UCR team has also explored another clever way to improve termite control: using scent to draw termites toward treated wood. In earlier work, Choe's lab studied pinene, a pleasant smelling chemical released by forest trees. To western drywood termites, pinene can signal food.

When termites follow that scent into insecticide treated wood, the treatment becomes more effective. A 2025 patent application from UCR describes the use of pinenes to improve localized insecticide injections against western drywood termites. It states that adding pinene to localized treatments killed termites more quickly and increased final mortality compared with insecticide alone. The application also suggests that pinene could allow wider spacing between drill holes and may reduce the time, labor, and amount of insecticide needed for treatment.

"We saw significant differences in the death rates using insecticide alone versus the insecticide plus pinene," said Choe. "Without pinene, we got about 70% mortality. When we added it in, it was over 95%."

Making It Practical for Homes

The bistrifluron study used acetone to dissolve the chemical before applying it to wood. That worked for the research, but it is not ideal for real world use because acetone is flammable and has a strong odor.

"We are working to make it more feasible for practical application in real life scenarios," Poulos said.

That next step matters because western drywood termites are a serious structural pest. They are native to northern Mexico and California, and they are especially important in California. The species has also been introduced to other regions, including Hawaii, New York, Florida, Canada, China, Japan, Korea, and Australia, according to the 2025 study. Movement of lumber and other wood products helps transport termites, while their concealed lifestyle inside wood makes them difficult to manage.

Climate change may add to the problem. As temperatures shift, the termites may be able to expand into places that were once less suitable for them.

"As we move lumber around the world, the termites are constantly transported to new locations. If they find the climate there acceptable, the problem will spread," Choe said. "In areas where these termites are common, it's just a matter of time before homes are infested, so this study is a good initial step toward alternative strategies for controlling them."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in termite control using a chemical that kills 95% of termites by targeting their molting process without harming humans or the environment. This method offers a safer, more targeted alternative to traditional toxic fumigation, with broad implications for protecting homes and reducing environmental harm. The research is detailed, focused on a single topic, and highlights potential large-scale benefits including reduced chemical toxicity, longer-lasting protection, and applicability in multiple regions affected by termite infestations.
articles.personality_title: New chemical kills 95% of termites without harming people
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Termites are insects that live hidden inside wooden parts of homes, slowly eating the wood and causing damage. They are hard to find and get rid of because they stay inside the wood and have a special way of growing called molting.

**What happened** – Scientists at the University of California, Riverside discovered a chemical named bistrifluron that stops termites from making a new hard outer shell during molting. Without this shell, termites die. Tests showed this chemical killed about 95% of drywood termites in a colony. It works slowly but safely, without the harmful effects of usual termite treatments.

**Impact** – This chemical is important because it targets termites specifically without hurting humans or pets. It spreads through termite colonies as termites share food, helping to kill the whole group even if only a few touch the chemical. Unlike traditional fumigation, it does not require people to leave their homes or remove food, making it safer and easier to use.

**What's next step** – Researchers are working to make the chemical easier to apply safely in real homes. They are also studying how adding a natural tree scent called pinene can attract termites to treated wood, making the treatment even more effective and needing less chemical.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists developed a safer, targeted chemical that kills most termites by blocking their growth, offering a better way to protect homes without harmful fumigation.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau produit chimique tue 95 % des termites sans nuire aux humains
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les termites vivent cachés dans le bois des maisons, où ils causent des dégâts en mangeant le bois. Ils sont difficiles à détecter et à éliminer car ils restent à l’intérieur du bois et grandissent en muant.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’Université de Californie à Riverside ont découvert une substance appelée bistrifluron qui empêche les termites de fabriquer une nouvelle carapace dure lors de la mue. Sans cette carapace, les termites meurent. Les tests ont montré que cette substance tuait environ 95 % des termites dans une colonie. Elle agit lentement mais en toute sécurité, sans les effets nocifs des traitements habituels.

**Impact** – Ce produit est important car il cible uniquement les termites sans nuire aux humains ni aux animaux domestiques. Il se propage dans la colonie grâce au partage de nourriture entre termites, ce qui permet de tuer tout le groupe même si seuls quelques termites entrent en contact avec la substance. Contrairement à la fumigation traditionnelle, il ne nécessite pas que les habitants quittent leur maison ou enlèvent leur nourriture, ce qui le rend plus sûr et plus facile à utiliser.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs travaillent à rendre ce produit plus facile à appliquer dans les maisons réelles. Ils étudient aussi comment ajouter une odeur naturelle d’arbre appelée pinène pour attirer les termites vers le bois traité, rendant le traitement encore plus efficace avec moins de produit.

**Phrase clé** – Des scientifiques ont mis au point un produit chimique sûr et ciblé qui bloque la croissance des termites et tue la plupart d’entre eux, offrant un meilleur moyen de protéger les maisons sans fumigation dangereuse.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo químico mata al 95 % de las termitas sin dañar a las personas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las termitas son insectos que viven escondidos dentro de la madera de las casas, donde causan daños al alimentarse. Son difíciles de detectar y eliminar porque permanecen dentro de la madera y crecen mudando su exoesqueleto.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad de California en Riverside descubrieron un químico llamado bistrifluron que impide que las termitas formen una nueva capa dura durante la muda. Sin esta capa, las termitas mueren. Las pruebas mostraron que este químico mató alrededor del 95 % de las termitas en una colonia. Funciona lentamente pero de manera segura, sin los efectos dañinos de los tratamientos tradicionales.

**Impacto** – Este químico es importante porque afecta solo a las termitas sin dañar a las personas ni a las mascotas. Se propaga por la colonia a medida que las termitas comparten comida, ayudando a eliminar todo el grupo aunque solo algunas hayan tocado el químico. A diferencia de la fumigación tradicional, no obliga a las personas a salir de sus casas ni a quitar la comida, por lo que es más seguro y fácil de usar.

**Qué sigue** – Los investigadores trabajan para que el químico sea más fácil de aplicar en casas reales. También estudian cómo añadir un aroma natural de árbol llamado pineno para atraer a las termitas hacia la madera tratada, haciendo el tratamiento más efectivo y con menos químico.

**Frase clave** – Científicos crearon un químico seguro y específico que bloquea el crecimiento de las termitas y mata a la mayoría, ofreciendo una mejor forma de proteger las casas sin fumigación dañina.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a wooden house cross-section showing a colony of small stylized termites inside, with delicate glowing threads symbolizing the spread of a gentle, protective chemical flowing between them, while a subtle soft light highlights the termites’ molting process being peacefully interrupted, all rendered in natural earthy tones and muted greens.

articles.title: A life in pictures: celebrating David Attenborough at 100
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 08 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01371-5As the British naturalist marks a milestone birthday, we take a look at how his work has shaped science.
articles.content: His advocacy has led to tangible results off screen. After the airing of his documentary Blue Planet II in 2017 — in which some of the episodes showed marine life being harmed by plastic — there was a surge in awareness around plastic waste, something that has been referred to as the Blue Planet Effect. There were anecdotal reports of people limiting their reliance on plastic after watching the documentary. Waitrose, a British supermarket chain headquartered in Bracknell, reported an 800% increase in consumers asking about plastic use after it aired. One study, which was focused on the UK political and media landscape, showed a rise in mentions of the issue in Parliament and in the news after the show1.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the significant positive impact of David Attenborough's documentary Blue Planet II on raising public and political awareness about plastic pollution, leading to measurable changes in consumer behavior and media focus, which contributes to environmental conservation efforts.
articles.personality_title: David Attenborough’s Blue Planet II sparks big change in plastic use
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – David Attenborough is a famous British naturalist known for making documentaries about nature. In 2017, his show Blue Planet II showed how plastic waste harms sea animals.

**What happened** – After Blue Planet II aired, many people started thinking more about how plastic affects the ocean. For example, the British supermarket Waitrose saw an 800% increase in customers asking about plastic use. Also, discussions about plastic pollution became more common in UK news and government meetings.

**Impact** – This change is important because it shows that a TV show can make people and leaders pay attention to environmental problems. When more people reduce plastic use, it helps protect animals and the ocean.

**What’s next step** – The increased awareness might lead to new rules or laws that limit plastic pollution. It could also inspire more people to choose products with less plastic.

**One-sentence takeaway** – David Attenborough’s Blue Planet II helped many people and leaders take plastic pollution more seriously, leading to clearer actions to protect the ocean.
articles.personality_title_fr: Blue Planet II de David Attenborough déclenche un grand changement dans l’utilisation du plastique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – David Attenborough est un naturaliste britannique célèbre pour ses documentaires sur la nature. En 2017, son émission Blue Planet II a montré comment les déchets plastiques nuisent aux animaux marins.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Après la diffusion de Blue Planet II, beaucoup de personnes ont commencé à réfléchir davantage à l’impact du plastique sur l’océan. Par exemple, le supermarché britannique Waitrose a vu une augmentation de 800 % des clients s’informant sur l’utilisation du plastique. De plus, les discussions sur la pollution plastique sont devenues plus fréquentes dans les médias et au Parlement britannique.

**Impact** – Ce changement est important car il montre qu’une émission télé peut faire prendre conscience aux gens et aux dirigeants des problèmes environnementaux. Quand plus de personnes réduisent leur usage du plastique, cela aide à protéger les animaux et l’océan.

**Prochaine étape** – Cette prise de conscience accrue pourrait mener à de nouvelles règles ou lois pour limiter la pollution plastique. Cela pourrait aussi encourager davantage de personnes à choisir des produits avec moins de plastique.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Blue Planet II de David Attenborough a aidé beaucoup de personnes et de dirigeants à prendre la pollution plastique plus au sérieux, ce qui conduit à des actions claires pour protéger l’océan.
articles.personality_title_es: Blue Planet II de David Attenborough impulsa un gran cambio en el uso del plástico
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – David Attenborough es un naturalista británico famoso por sus documentales sobre la naturaleza. En 2017, su programa Blue Planet II mostró cómo los desechos plásticos dañan a los animales marinos.

**Qué pasó** – Después de la emisión de Blue Planet II, muchas personas comenzaron a pensar más en cómo el plástico afecta al océano. Por ejemplo, el supermercado británico Waitrose vio un aumento del 800 % en clientes que preguntaban sobre el uso de plástico. También, las discusiones sobre la contaminación plástica se hicieron más comunes en las noticias y en el Parlamento del Reino Unido.

**Impacto** – Este cambio es importante porque muestra que un programa de televisión puede hacer que la gente y los líderes presten atención a los problemas ambientales. Cuando más personas reducen el uso de plástico, ayuda a proteger a los animales y al océano.

**Próximo paso** – Esta mayor conciencia podría llevar a nuevas reglas o leyes para limitar la contaminación plástica. También podría inspirar a más personas a elegir productos con menos plástico.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Blue Planet II de David Attenborough ayudó a muchas personas y líderes a tomar más en serio la contaminación por plástico, lo que lleva a acciones claras para proteger el océano.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a vibrant ocean scene where diverse marine animals swim freely around a large, gently glowing blue planet-shaped coral reef, with delicate strands of discarded plastic waste dissolving into the water like fading threads, symbolizing the positive impact of awareness on plastic pollution.

articles.title: A vast dam across the Bering Strait could stop the AMOC collapsing
articles.summary: If a key ocean current collapses it could plunge northern Europe into a big freeze. Now researchers are weighing up a drastic intervention – building a 130-kilometre-wide dam between the US and Russia
articles.content:
If a key ocean current collapses it could plunge northern Europe into a big freeze. Now researchers are weighing up a drastic intervention – building a 130-kilometre-wide dam between the US and Russia

The Bering Strait separates Alaska and Russia Ocean Color/OB.DAAC/OBPG/NASA

It would be an engineering project on a truly epic scale, but we may one day need to consider building a dam between Alaska and eastern Russia. The audacious proposal would be designed to stave off the worst consequences of the collapse of a vital ocean current, and researchers have been mulling it over this week at a major conference.

The idea comes from Jelle Soons and his colleague Henk Dijkstra at the University of Utrecht in the Netherlands, who study the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation or AMOC. This current system, which includes the Gulf Stream, is a major reason why northern Europe has a relatively mild climate for its latitude.

However, we know the current is weakening. There is huge uncertainty about what would happen if it collapses, but some models suggest it could see temperatures in northern Europe drastically plunge.

Soons thought a dam could be a possible intervention after hearing about how during the Pliocene era, from roughly 5.3 to 2.6 million years ago, sea levels were lower and there was a land bridge where we now find the Bering Strait. Simulations of the Pliocene climate show the AMOC was stronger then, mainly thanks to that land bridge. “I was like: ok, could we do this again?” says Soons.

To investigate the effects of building such a dam, Soons and Dijkstra ran simulations of the AMOC varying both the date when the dam would be built and the exact amount of freshwater present.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Earth Edition Unmissable news about our planet, delivered straight to your inbox each month. Sign up to newsletter

Freshwater is a key part of the equation because it currently flows from the Pacific through the Bering Strait into the north Atlantic, which weakens the AMOC. Building a dam would stop or slow the flow.

In work published a few weeks ago, Soons and Dijkstra obtained mixed results: in some scenarios the dam appeared to strengthen the AMOC, but in others it had the opposite effect. However, those results came from a relatively simple and low-resolution model.

On 5 May at the European Geosciences Union general assembly in Vienna, Austria, Soons presented work that repeated the simulations on a supercomputer using a much more advanced climate model. This indicated that closing the Strait would strengthen AMOC, especially if the dam were built early – by at least 2050. “I was surprised at how strong the recovery was,” says Soons.

The Bering Strait is only 59 metres deep at its deepest point and there are two small islands in the middle, meaning any barrier could conceivably be built in two halves. Ed McCann, a past president of the Institution of Civil Engineers and now at Expedition Engineering says the best way to do this would be to avoid concrete and instead use floating machinery to build a barrier of rock and dredged sand. “This sort of construction is pretty simple, just very big and very expensive,” he wrote in an email.

Jonathan Rosser at the London School of Economics says that the work is interesting but that because we don’t fully understand the AMOC, we can’t be sure of the consequences of such an intervention. “These drastic things really do have big uncertainties attached.”

Soons agrees and says that while building a dam might be helpful to northern Europe, it could create other problems, such as altering rainfall patterns, elsewhere. “Whether you would consider this a serious proposal? I don’t think we’re there yet,” he says.

This is not the first time that researchers have mulled the idea of building a huge sea dam to mitigate climate change. In 2020, Sjoerd Groeskamp at the Royal Netherlands Institute for Sea Research unveiled an idea called the Northern European Enclosure Dam, which would involve building two barriers to hem in the sea between the UK and Europe and prevent rising sea levels from inundating low-lying parts of the continent.

As well as effects on climate, any such dam would have other side effects on things like marine-mammal migrations, tides and shipping to remote communities. Soons says he has toyed with ideas like building half a barrier or having it descend to a depth of only say 10 metres. These are “interesting ideas” he says, although he hasn’t yet had a chance to consider their merits properly.
articles.reasoning: The article discusses a significant scientific proposal to build a large-scale dam in the Bering Strait to prevent the collapse of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC), which would have major climate benefits by potentially averting a severe freeze in northern Europe. The proposal is based on advanced climate modeling and has broad implications for climate action and environmental stability. While the idea is still speculative and carries uncertainties, it represents a meaningful and substantive potential intervention with large-scale positive impact on climate resilience.
articles.personality_title: Scientists explore building a dam across the Bering Strait to protect Europe’s climate
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) is an important ocean current that helps keep northern Europe’s climate mild. Recently, scientists have found that this current is weakening, which could cause much colder weather in northern Europe.

**What happened** – Researchers from the University of Utrecht suggested building a large dam across the Bering Strait, the narrow waterway between Alaska and Russia, to help keep the AMOC strong. They used advanced computer models to test the idea and found that closing the strait with a dam could strengthen the current, especially if built before 2050. The strait is about 130 kilometers wide and only 59 meters deep at its deepest point, which makes building a dam possible, though very expensive and challenging.

**Impact** – This idea is unique because it tries to use a big engineering project to solve a problem caused by climate change. If successful, it could prevent northern Europe from becoming much colder. However, the scientists warn that such a dam could change weather patterns in other parts of the world and affect marine life and shipping routes.

**What's next step** – Scientists will need to study the possible effects more carefully to understand both the benefits and risks. They will also explore different designs, like smaller barriers or partial dams, to find safer ways to help the AMOC without causing other problems.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Building a dam across the Bering Strait is a bold idea that might protect Europe’s climate by strengthening an important ocean current but requires more research to understand its full effects.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques envisagent de construire un barrage dans le détroit de Béring pour protéger le climat européen
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La circulation méridionale de retournement atlantique (AMOC) est un courant océanique important qui aide à maintenir un climat doux en Europe du Nord. Récemment, les scientifiques ont constaté que ce courant s’affaiblit, ce qui pourrait entraîner un refroidissement important en Europe du Nord.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université d’Utrecht ont proposé de construire un grand barrage dans le détroit de Béring, l’étroit passage entre l’Alaska et la Russie, pour aider à renforcer l’AMOC. Ils ont utilisé des modèles informatiques avancés qui montrent que fermer le détroit avec un barrage pourrait renforcer le courant, surtout s’il est construit avant 2050. Le détroit mesure environ 130 kilomètres de large et seulement 59 mètres de profondeur à son point le plus profond, ce qui rend la construction possible, mais coûteuse et difficile.

**Impact** – Cette idée est unique car elle utilise un grand projet d’ingénierie pour résoudre un problème causé par le changement climatique. Si elle réussit, elle pourrait empêcher un refroidissement important en Europe du Nord. Cependant, les scientifiques avertissent que ce barrage pourrait modifier les conditions météorologiques ailleurs et affecter la vie marine et les voies de navigation.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques devront étudier plus en détail les effets possibles pour mieux comprendre les avantages et les risques. Ils examineront aussi différents designs, comme des barrières plus petites ou des barrages partiels, pour trouver des solutions plus sûres afin d’aider l’AMOC sans créer d’autres problèmes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Construire un barrage dans le détroit de Béring est une idée audacieuse qui pourrait protéger le climat européen en renforçant un courant océanique important, mais qui nécessite encore des recherches pour en comprendre tous les effets.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos estudian construir una presa en el estrecho de Bering para proteger el clima europeo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Circulación Meridional de Vuelco del Atlántico (AMOC) es una corriente oceánica importante que ayuda a mantener un clima suave en el norte de Europa. Recientemente, los científicos han descubierto que esta corriente se está debilitando, lo que podría causar un enfriamiento fuerte en esa región.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Utrecht propusieron construir una gran presa en el estrecho de Bering, el paso estrecho entre Alaska y Rusia, para ayudar a fortalecer la AMOC. Usaron modelos informáticos avanzados que muestran que cerrar el estrecho con una presa podría fortalecer la corriente, especialmente si se construye antes de 2050. El estrecho tiene unos 130 kilómetros de ancho y solo 59 metros de profundidad en su punto más profundo, lo que hace posible la construcción, aunque costosa y complicada.

**Impacto** – Esta idea es única porque intenta usar un gran proyecto de ingeniería para solucionar un problema causado por el cambio climático. Si tiene éxito, podría evitar que el norte de Europa se enfríe mucho. Sin embargo, los científicos advierten que una presa así podría cambiar los patrones climáticos en otras partes del mundo y afectar la vida marina y las rutas de navegación.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos necesitarán estudiar más a fondo los posibles efectos para comprender mejor los beneficios y riesgos. También explorarán diferentes diseños, como barreras más pequeñas o presas parciales, para encontrar formas más seguras de ayudar a la AMOC sin causar otros problemas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Construir una presa en el estrecho de Bering es una idea audaz que podría proteger el clima europeo fortaleciendo una corriente oceánica importante, pero requiere más investigación para entender todos sus efectos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vast, gently curving dam made of natural rock and sand stretching across the calm, shallow waters of the Bering Strait between silhouetted coastlines of Alaska and Russia, with soft currents flowing around it symbolizing the redirected ocean flow, under a serene sky with subtle hints of northern lights to evoke the connection to northern Europe's climate.
